title: Payroll Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541214 ($22,102,632,622) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541214 ($27,322,843,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $98,054 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541214); class <5: 2,110 firms, avg revenue/firm $581,493 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $91,004 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 662 firms, avg revenue/firm $919,816 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $143,951 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 480 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,588,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $248,602 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 741 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,599,022 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $719,747 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 297 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,560,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,122,240 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 194 firms, avg revenue/firm $89,119,103 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,947,140 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 297
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.role: Payroll processing specialists / payroll and timekeeping clerks
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Core AI/RPA territory: data entry, exception flagging, tax-notice research, off-cycle runs, and W-2 generation are well within current AI capability. Symmetry 2026 survey confirms 35% of payroll orgs already use AI for data entry/error detection; already shrinking head count per client at ADP/Paychex. Fraction 0.65 leaves 0.35 for multi-state garnishment adjudication, agency notice resolution, and client-liaison exception handling that requires human judgment.

Tier-1 inquiries (pay stub questions, portal logins, standard deduction queries) automatable via AI chat. Escalations involving money movement, tax-agency notices, and garnishments keep humans; only partial automation credit. 0.35 reflects ~1/3 of interactions being tier-1.
Accountant-referral channel and direct SMB sales are relationship-driven; near-zero AI replaceability. AI may assist with CRM qualification but the sales relationship is irreplaceable.

Data migration and form setup increasingly tool-assisted; guided onboarding wizards reduce human hours. Complex multi-state setup, garnishment enrollment, and prior-period corrections persist. Fraction 0.30 reflects the tool-assisted portion.

AI-augmented coding (Copilot-class tools) and monitoring automation save time but do not eliminate the role. Independent bureaus have minimal dev staff; platform players dominate. Small automatable fraction for documentation, test generation, and monitoring alerts.

Tax-remittance compliance sign-off, NACHA money-movement authorization, and client relationships are not automatable. Small fraction (0.15) for AI-assisted reporting, anomaly detection, and regulatory-change summarization.

pi_dist.rationale: Independent bureaus nominally own the client relationship, but the accountant-referral channel (which controls the SMB payroll decision for many small firms) increasingly routes to Intuit/Gusto partner programs, reducing bureau power. Embedded fintech (Square Payroll, Gusto at POS/banking) owns the banking relationship and undercuts PEPM. Switching costs exist (tax IDs, direct-deposit routing, prior-period records) but are lower than CPA firm switching costs. PEPM pricing is subject to deflationary pressure from platform competitors publishing visible prices. pi_dist set at 0.45: bureaus own some clients but share the relationship with upstream accountant channel and embedded platforms.
sources: Gusto partner program structure (documented in payroll market analyses)

pi_moat.rationale: IRS reporting-agent status, NACHA money-movement credentials, state tax-remittance accounts, and multi-year client data create real switching costs. However: (1) no client-side licensure requirement (any employer can move payroll at fiscal-year-end), (2) platform oligopoly (ADP/Paychex) competes on price and scale advantages independent bureaus cannot match, (3) embedded fintech platforms own the banking relationship for SMBs and can add payroll at near-zero marginal cost, (4) client-funds float income unavailable to independents is a structural disadvantage. Moat is moderate-low: regulatory barriers prevent easy new entry but do not prevent client migration to large platforms.
sources: ADP/Paychex scale discussion in industry analyses

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption baseline: Symmetry June 2026 survey (300 payroll/compliance professionals): 78% already using or piloting AI — but this pool skews heavily toward software vendors and large-firm payroll teams. For independent payroll bureaus (the acquirable pool, n=4,484), adoption is significantly lower; PayrollOrg projects 50% AI adoption for payroll organizations by end-of-2025 broadly. Accounting for small-bureau lag, I estimate ~35% of firms in 541214 are materially using AI (data entry automation, error detection) as of July 2026, consistent with Census AI use data showing <20% of firms with <5 employees. Cloud payroll adoption analog: from 2009 (~5% cloud) to 2021 (~55% cloud) was ~12 years to cross 50%. AI is moving ~2-3x faster than cloud adoption due to API accessibility and lower capital cost. From 35% baseline, reaching 50% requires ~15pp gain. At observed AI adoption acceleration (historical cloud: ~4pp/yr; AI: ~8-10pp/yr), the 50% threshold is ~1.5-2 years away on optimistic scenario, ~5-6 years on small-bureau conservative scenario. Central estimate: 4 years (2030) to account for the long tail of small independent bureaus.

current_adoption_pct.source: Symmetry / PayrollOrg surveys (2025-2026), calibrated to independent bureau segment
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 78% of payroll and compliance teams are already living with AI in some form (Symmetry June 2026 survey, 300 professionals). PayrollOrg 2025 projects ~50% payroll org adoption by end-2025. Calibrated down to ~35% for small independent bureaus given Census data showing <20% of <5-employee firms use AI and the acquirable pool is predominantly small.

historical_analogs.value: Cloud payroll adoption: ~12 years from ~5% (2009) to ~65% enterprise (2023); AI adoption moving ~2-3x faster per observed data
historical_analogs.source: Precedence Research cloud payroll market report; Census AI use data 2026
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 65% of enterprises adopted cloud payroll by end of 2023 (Precedence Research); cloud payroll CAGR 11.8% 2024-2025

owners_60plus_pct.source: Census Annual Business Survey 2022; Federal Reserve Small Business Credit Survey 2025
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 52.3% of US business owners are 55 or older (2022 ABS): 29.5% in 55-64 cohort, 22.8% age 65+. Estimating 60+ as half of 55-64 cohort (14.75%) plus all 65+ (22.8%) = ~37.55%, rounded to 0.38. No payroll-bureau-specific owner-age data available.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Gallup 2024: 78% of small business owners lack a formal succession plan. Fed Minneapolis 2023: boomer owners hanging on past retirement age. Fed SBCS 2025: 62% of owners have seen retirement timelines accelerate. Project Equity: 2.9M employer firms owned by 55+ owners face succession pressure.

active_consolidators.source: Press releases and PrivSource deal tracking; industry news 2024-2025
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: MPAY/Payentry: 3 acquisitions in 2024 (Payroll Resource Group Apr, Southland Data Processing Jul, Web Payroll & Tax Solutions Oct) + CPS merger 2026. ConnectPay: 27 acquisitions since 2017 (4-7/yr). Payroll Growth Partners: active 2022-present. Paper Trails: acquired Local Economy Payroll. Paybridge: acquired School Payroll Services (via Payroll Growth Partners). Counting MPAY, ConnectPay, Payroll Growth Partners, Paper Trails, Paybridge as 5 distinct active PE-backed/growth-equity consolidators doing add-ons in the last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: BizBuySell service business multiples; accounting/payroll practice broker data; ConnectPay acquisition model
buy_mult.figure_quoted: BizBuySell 2024: median SDE multiple all industries 2.57x; service businesses 2.0-3.3x SDE. Accounting/tax practices: 3.5-5.5x SDE for mid-size. Converting SDE to EBITDA (add back market-rate owner comp ~$80K): for a small bureau with $300K SDE, implied EBITDA ~$220K; the EBITDA multiple is higher than SDE multiple. For the 100-499 employee class (the n_band: avg EBITDA ~$2.1M), acquirers pay 4-6x EBITDA based on recurring payroll revenue (PEPM base = subscription-like). Conservative buy_mult = 4.0x EBITDA after normalization.

exit_mult.source: Alight payroll & professional services sale (H.I.G. Capital 2024); HCM sector M&A analysis
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Alight sold payroll & professional services segment to H.I.G. Capital affiliate for up to $1.2B, valued at approximately 10x estimated 2023 adjusted EBITDA. Raymond James HCM report: pure-play HCM shops 6-8x EBITDA, financially-integrated HCM 10-14x. Business services platform median 10.7x (mid-2026). Payroll platforms with $10M+ EBITDA and recurring PEPM revenue command 8-12x. Central estimate: 10.0x.

pricing_structure.value: Per-employee-per-month (PEPM) plus per-run fees; subscription-like recurring revenue model. ADP small-business: $79/mo base + ~$4 PEPM; Workforce Now $19-28 PEPM. Independent bureaus typically price at $20-40 PEPM with per-run and year-end fees. Small bureaus also earn ancillary revenue from workers comp, HR modules, and tax filing services.
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: ADP RUN starts at $79/month + $4 PEPM for small businesses (1-49 employees); ADP Workforce Now $19-28/employee/month. Platform competition sets effective price ceilings for independents.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.33853, so AI could free 33.85pp of revenue as labor cost. EBITDA uplift = 33.85pp (treating freed labor as direct margin improvement on revenue basis). A = 2.5 (< 5), so the uplift < 3pp tension rule does not apply here. The uplift is very large (33.85pp) and A is low (2.5 = t50 of 4 years), which is consistent: the industry has large AI value potential but moderate-slow adoption speed for the long tail of independent small bureaus.

terminal_value.justification: Payroll intermediaries face two structural pressures: (1) AI cuts the bureau's internal processing cost but does NOT eliminate the reason for a paid intermediary — IRS reporting-agent status, NACHA money-movement authorization, state tax-remittance accounts, garnishment liability, and multi-state compliance complexity preserve a real compliance and custody function that AI cannot directly replace. (2) However, the platform oligopoly (ADP/Paychex with float income) and embedded fintech (Gusto, Square, banking-embedded payroll) are the primary beneficiaries, not the acquirable independent bureau. AI agents filing directly with IRS/state systems would require NACHA and money-transmission compliance that independents would still be needed to provide — but the economics may concentrate further at platforms. Terminal verdict: PRESSURED. Independent payroll bureaus' demand shrinks as clients migrate to embedded platforms and platform consolidation, but does not go to zero because multi-state complexity, garnishment, and compliance-agent liability still require a paid intermediary. Distinguish: AI cutting a bureau's processing cost (likely, saves labor) vs. AI dissolving the bureau's role (less likely in 5-10 years given regulatory structure). Demand persists but shifts to oligopoly.
terminal_value.justification_full: PRESSURED: compliance custody and multi-state complexity preserve the intermediary role but economics concentrate at ADP/Paychex/embedded platforms rather than acquirable independent bureaus.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V scored from labor-cost replacement analysis of specific occupational functions (payroll clerks, CS reps, implementation staff) — no reference to deal activity. A scored from PayrollOrg/Symmetry AI adoption surveys and cloud-payroll historical analog — no reference to PE consolidation or M&A trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Payroll is billed PEPM (subscription-like), which is in principle deflation-resistant per unit. However: (1) competitive platforms (Gusto ~$6/employee, embedded payroll often bundled at near-zero) publish visible price points that cap what independents can charge; (2) when AI cuts processing labor, competitive pressure from these platform price points forces independents to pass savings to clients rather than keep as margin; (3) this is modeled via pi_dist = 0.45 (shared client control with upstream accountant channel and embedded platforms) and pi_moat = 0.40 (switching costs exist but insufficient to hold pricing premium vs. platforms). Both inputs are depressed from their theoretical maximum to reflect the deflationary pass-through mechanism.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = min(10, 10/t50): at t50=2 yrs A=5.0, S~5.5; at t50=7 yrs A=1.43, S~3.8. The 2-7 yr range shifts S from ~3.8 to ~5.5.
C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat: at pi_dist=0.35 C=1.4, at pi_dist=0.60 C=2.4. Shifts S from ~4.2 to ~4.8.

M = clamp(4×(10-buy)/buy, 0, 10): at buy=3.0 M=9.33→capped 10; at buy=6.0 M=2.67. Shifts S from ~5.0 (if buy=3) to ~4.0 (if buy=6). Significant uncertainty — no direct payroll-bureau EBITDA comp set found.

reviewer_note: Role-mix warning: the dataset's 4-digit 5412 OEWS mix is CPA-firm dominated (13-2011 Accountants 34.2% of wages) and omits the signature occupation of 541214 (43-3051 Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks, not in the top-10). ai_replaceable_share was built from the prompt's hypothesis role structure (payroll processing specialists 40%, CS 17.5%, sales 12.5%, implementation 10%, IT 10%, management 10% of wage bill) rather than the inapplicable 4-digit OEWS. Labor_share = 0.8625 is extremely high partly due to vintage mismatch (2024 wages over 2022 receipts) and partly because bureau revenue nets out pass-through client funds; V = 10 (ceiling) reflects this — treat with caution. n_band = 297 (100-499 employee class only) understates the acquirable pool if smaller firms with EBITDA in band exist — the dataset methodology puts them out of band but real broker data may disagree. buy_mult ESTIMATE quality due to absence of direct payroll-bureau EBITDA comp set; converting from SDE-basis broker data. terminal_value = PRESSURED: the intermediary role survives AI but economics concentrate at ADP/Paychex/embedded platforms rather than the acquirable independent bureau pool.
